mission.title: Install and Configure K3s (Lightweight Kubernetes)
mission.description: K3s is a lightweight Kubernetes distribution designed for resource-constrained environments, such as edge computing and IoT. It simplifies the installation and management of Kubernetes by packaging it as a single binary, making it ideal for users who need a production-ready Kubernetes cluster without the overhead of a full Kubernetes installation. Key trade-offs include reduced features compared to full Kubernetes and reliance on a single binary.

steps.title: Install K3s Binary
steps.description:
This step installs the K3s binary on your system, which is the core component of K3s.

```bash

curl -sfL https://get.k3s.io | sh -

```

> This command downloads and installs the latest K3s version.

steps.title: Verify K3s Installation
steps.description:
After installation, verify that K3s is running correctly.

```bash

sudo k3s kubectl get nodes

```

> This command checks the status of the nodes in your K3s cluster.

steps.title: Configure Kubeconfig
steps.description:
Set up the kubeconfig file to interact with your K3s cluster using kubectl.

```bash

sudo k3s kubectl config view --raw > ~/.kube/config

```

> This command saves the K3s kubeconfig to your home directory.

steps.title: Deploy a Sample Application
steps.description:
Deploy a simple application to verify that your K3s cluster is functioning as expected.

```bash

kubectl create deployment nginx --image=nginx

kubectl expose deployment nginx --port=80 --type=NodePort

```

> This command deploys an Nginx server.
> This command exposes the Nginx deployment.

steps.title: Verify Application Deployment
steps.description:
Check that the application is running and accessible.

```bash

kubectl get services

```

> This command lists the services to find the NodePort assigned to the Nginx service.

uninstall.title: Uninstall K3s
uninstall.description:
This step removes K3s from your system. Be aware that this will delete all resources in the cluster.

```bash

sudo /usr/local/bin/k3s-uninstall.sh

```

> This command uninstalls K3s and cleans up all associated resources.

upgrade.title: Upgrade K3s
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your cluster state. This step upgrades K3s to the latest version.

```bash

sudo k3s upgrade

kubectl get nodes

```

> This command upgrades K3s to the latest version.
> Verify that all nodes are in a Ready state after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'k3s is not running'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** K3s service may not have started correctly.

**Fix:**
Check the K3s service status with 'sudo systemctl status k3s'. If it's not running, restart it with 'sudo systemctl restart k3s'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'kubectl: command not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Kubeconfig may not be set up correctly.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the kubeconfig file is in place with 'sudo k3s kubectl config view --raw > ~/.kube/config'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to connect to the server'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** K3s server may not be running or kubeconfig is incorrect.

**Fix:**
Check K3s server status and verify kubeconfig settings.

troubleshooting.title: Pods are in 'Pending' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Insufficient resources available in the cluster.

**Fix:**
Check node resources with 'kubectl describe nodes' and ensure there are enough resources for the pods.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new CNI plugins, improved metrics server integration.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated support for certain legacy storage backends.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v1.30, ensure to update your storage configurations.

resolution.summary: You should see the output of 'kubectl get nodes' showing the nodes in a Ready state, indicating that K3s is running correctly.

prerequisites.description: Ensure your system meets the minimum resource requirements for K3s.
